heroOverview: Enteroptosis is the abnormal downward displacement of one or more loops of the small intestine within the abdominal cavity, caused by laxity of the mesenteric and peritoneal ligaments that normally suspend the gut in its anatomical position. It sits within the broader category of visceroptosis — prolapse of abdominal organs — first described systematically in the early twentieth century and still debated as a clinical entity. While enteroptosis is visible on upright abdominal imaging, it is frequently asymptomatic; when it does cause symptoms, patients typically report chronic lower abdominal heaviness, bloating, constipation, and a dragging aching sensation that worsens with prolonged standing. Prevalence data are limited, but enteroptosis is disproportionately reported in thin women with poor abdominal wall tone and in individuals who have undergone rapid weight loss. Most patients are managed with conservative measures including abdominal strengthening exercises, dietary adjustments, and physical support, and surgical correction is rarely required.

keyStats.prevalence: Prevalence data are limited; early twentieth century abdominal radiography studies estimated visceroptosis in 15-20% of adults, though modern imaging series suggest clinically significant cases are much less common
keyStats.demographics: More frequently reported in women than men; disproportionate among thin adults with low abdominal wall muscle mass and those who have experienced rapid or substantial weight loss
keyStats.avgAge: Most symptomatic presentations occur in adults aged 20-50
keyStats.globalCases: No reliable global incidence data; considered a relatively rare cause of abdominal symptoms in current gastroenterology practice

definition: Enteroptosis (ICD-10: K63.4) is the downward sagging or ptosis of the small intestinal loops within the peritoneal cavity below their normal anatomical position, resulting from weakness or elongation of the mesentery, suspensory ligaments, or peritoneal folds that ordinarily anchor the bowel. The condition belongs to the broader concept of visceroptosis — also termed Glenard's disease after the French physician who first described the syndrome in 1885 — in which any abdominal organ may be displaced downward under the influence of gravity, especially in the upright position. Enteroptosis may be congenital (associated with abnormally long mesentery) or acquired (following rapid weight loss, prolonged illness, or connective tissue laxity). The diagnosis falls under gastroenterology, though it overlaps with general surgery and pelvic floor medicine when it coexists with uterine or pelvic organ prolapse. Clinically, enteroptosis exists on a spectrum: isolated imaging findings without symptoms at one end, and a chronic functional bowel syndrome with postural pain, bloating, constipation, and intermittent partial obstruction at the other. The clinical significance of the imaging finding and its causal relationship to symptoms must be carefully evaluated before attributing symptoms to enteroptosis, as many patients with visible intestinal displacement on imaging are asymptomatic.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, occurring without an identifiable structural cause; typically in constitutionally thin individuals with low abdominal wall muscle tone and long, lax mesentery.

Rapid or substantial weight loss reduces the omental and mesenteric fat that partly cushions and supports the intestinal loops, allowing the gut to descend; common after bariatric surgery or severe illness.
Abnormally long mesentery or congenitally lax peritoneal ligaments allow early-life bowel ptosis; often first identified incidentally on imaging in young adults.
Stretching of the anterior abdominal wall and pelvic floor during pregnancy and delivery reduces support for the abdominal organs; may coexist with uterine or bladder prolapse.
Enteroptosis occurring alongside downward displacement of other organs — gastroptosis (stomach), nephroptosis (kidney), or hepatoptosis (liver) — within the broader Glenard's syndrome.

primarySymptoms: Chronic lower abdominal heaviness or a dragging, pulling sensation that is most noticeable when standing or walking and relieves when lying down.
Abdominal bloating and distension, worsening through the day and with prolonged upright posture as the gut descends under gravity.
Constipation or difficulty defecating, caused by mechanical kinking or slowing of intestinal transit when loops descend and mesenteric tension alters bowel tone.
Lower abdominal or pelvic cramping pain, often dull and chronic, attributed to mesenteric traction and bowel distension.
Nausea, particularly after meals, as the displaced stomach and small bowel alter gastric emptying and intestinal motility.
Early satiety or a feeling of fullness after eating small amounts, from gastroptosis that frequently coexists with enteroptosis.
Postural variation in symptoms — a reliable feature — with marked worsening in the standing or walking position and prompt relief in supine or Trendelenburg position.
Visible protrusion or bulging of the lower abdomen in the standing position, which reduces or disappears on lying down.
Fatigue and lethargy from chronic pain, sleep disruption, and the metabolic cost of poor nutrition related to early satiety.
Occasional diarrhoea alternating with constipation in some patients, reflecting disordered bowel motility from mesenteric traction.
earlyWarningSigns: Gradually increasing lower abdominal discomfort and heaviness through the day that was not present one to two years ago.
Noticing that abdominal bloating reliably eases when lying flat, suggesting a postural mechanical component.
Worsening constipation without change in diet in a recently weight-reduced patient.
A sense that the lower abdomen bulges more when standing after substantial weight loss.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe abdominal pain with vomiting and failure to pass gas or stool — possible acute intestinal obstruction from a volvulus of a displaced loop, requiring emergency assessment.
Abdominal pain with fever, rigidity, and rebound tenderness — possible bowel strangulation, a surgical emergency.
Rapid clinical deterioration, hypotension, or vomiting of faeculent material in a patient with known enteroptosis.

causes.cause: Constitutional laxity of the mesentery and peritoneal ligaments
causes.description: In some individuals, the mesentery — the double-layered peritoneal fold from which the intestines are suspended — is inherently longer or more lax than average, allowing the intestinal loops to descend; this constitutional tendency is more pronounced in asthenic (thin-framed) body types.

The omentum and mesenteric fat pads normally fill the peritoneal cavity and provide a cushion supporting visceral position; rapid loss of this fat — from illness, extreme dieting, or bariatric surgery — removes the support and allows the intestines to descend gravitationally.

Weak anterior abdominal muscles fail to provide adequate intra-abdominal pressure to resist gravity-driven organ descent; this is seen after prolonged bed rest, deconditioning, multiple pregnancies, and in individuals with low muscle mass from chronic illness.

Hypermobility spectrum disorders, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hypermobile type), and Marfan syndrome feature generalised ligamentous laxity that extends to the peritoneal folds, predisposing to organ ptosis across multiple body systems.

causes.cause: Post-partum changes to pelvic floor and abdominal wall
causes.description: Pregnancy stretches and weakens the anterior abdominal wall and pelvic floor; the resulting loss of structural support can persist after delivery, particularly after multiple pregnancies or large babies, allowing the intestines to descend more than usual.

Extended periods of bed rest or severe illness produce substantial loss of abdominal muscle mass (sarcopaenia) and ligamentous tone; recovered patients may find that previously asymptomatic enteroptosis becomes symptomatic when they return to upright activity.

riskFactors.factor: Asthenic body habitus (thin, low muscle mass)
riskFactors.description: Constitutional thinness reduces both the mesenteric fat cushion and the abdominal muscle tone that resist intestinal descent; this body type was classically associated with visceroptosis in early medical literature.

Women are reported to have enteroptosis more frequently than men, possibly related to greater constitutional ligamentous laxity, multiple pregnancies, and the anatomical proximity of the pelvic floor, all of which reduce visceral support.
Substantial weight reduction — particularly more than 20-30 kg over months — removes mesenteric fat support; gradual weight reduction and concurrent abdominal strengthening partially mitigate this.
Each pregnancy progressively stretches the abdominal wall and pelvic ligaments; grand multiparous women have higher rates of pelvic organ prolapse and visceroptosis.
Generalised ligamentous laxity caused by collagen structure abnormalities allows abnormal mobility and sagging of all peritoneal-supported viscera, not just the bowel.

Occupations requiring prolonged upright standing increase the daily gravitational load on the mesenteric suspensory structures; postural muscle fatigue removes the dynamic support that walking and muscle contraction otherwise provide.
Adhesions and scar tissue from prior laparotomy or laparoscopy can tether bowel in abnormal positions and alter mesenteric tension, potentially aggravating symptoms of enteroptosis.

affectedDemographics: Thin women aged 20-50, the most consistently reported demographic in case series
Patients after rapid or substantial weight loss, including post-bariatric surgery patients
Individuals with connective tissue hypermobility disorders
Women with pelvic organ prolapse, where pelvic floor laxity often coexists
Adults recovering from prolonged illness or extended bed rest with significant muscle loss
diagnosisOverview: Enteroptosis is principally an imaging diagnosis confirmed in the context of compatible symptoms. An upright abdominal X-ray or CT scan of the abdomen performed with the patient standing demonstrates small intestinal loops lying below a line connecting the two iliac crests — a position abnormal in comparison to the supine image of the same patient. Contrast studies (barium follow-through or CT enterography) provide additional detail about transit, mesenteric attachments, and the presence of adhesions or partial obstruction. The key clinical finding is postural variation: symptoms that are clearly worse upright and promptly relieved by lying down or applying an abdominal support belt. Gastroenterologists should carefully exclude other explanations before attributing symptoms to enteroptosis: irritable bowel syndrome, functional dyspepsia, chronic constipation, adhesion-related obstruction, and coeliac disease can all produce similar complaints. Colonoscopy or sigmoidoscopy is not diagnostic of enteroptosis but excludes luminal pathology. Pelvic floor assessment by a gynaecologist or urogynecologist is valuable when coexisting pelvic organ prolapse is suspected. MRI or cine MRI of the abdomen can demonstrate dynamic bowel movement and mesenteric mobility in specialist centres.

diagnosticTests.test: Upright and supine abdominal imaging (X-ray or CT)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates the downward displacement of intestinal loops in the standing position compared to supine; the key diagnostic comparison confirming postural organ descent.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Plain X-ray is a brief procedure with minimal radiation; CT involves an injection of contrast dye and passage through the scanner over 5-10 minutes. Both are performed first supine and then upright.

diagnosticTests.test: Barium follow-through or small bowel meal
diagnosticTests.purpose: Traces the contrast agent through the small intestine to map transit, identify adhesions, partial obstruction, or abnormal mesenteric mobility contributing to symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You drink a chalky barium liquid and sequential X-rays are taken over 1-4 hours as the barium travels through the intestine; no needles or radiation-significant procedure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: High-resolution cross-sectional imaging of the small bowel in the upright position to map intestinal position, mesenteric anatomy, transit speed, and exclude luminal disease or adhesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A large volume of oral contrast is drunk over 45-60 minutes before the scan; an IV contrast injection is also given; the scan itself takes 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes structural luminal pathology — polyps, inflammation, strictures — that could cause the same symptoms before attributing them to enteroptosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Colonoscopy requires a bowel preparation the day before; the procedure takes 20-40 minutes under sedation. Upper endoscopy takes 10-20 minutes under sedation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes coeliac disease as an alternative explanation for bloating, abdominal pain, and altered bowel habit in any patient with these symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies coexisting pelvic organ prolapse, which shares risk factors and exacerbates abdominal symptoms in women; guides physiotherapy referral.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinical examination by a gynaecologist or pelvic floor physiotherapist; may include real-time pelvic floor ultrasound or MRI defecography in complex cases.

treatmentOverview: The overwhelming majority of patients with enteroptosis are managed conservatively, as surgery is rarely indicated and carries substantial risk without guaranteed benefit. The cornerstone of conservative management is strengthening the abdominal wall musculature through structured physical therapy focused on core stabilisation and postural exercises, which increase intra-abdominal pressure and reduce gravitational organ descent. An abdominal support belt or compression garment worn during prolonged standing provides immediate symptomatic relief by mechanically raising the displaced intestines and is a practical short-term measure. Dietary modification addresses the associated constipation and bloating: a fibre-appropriate diet with adequate hydration, small frequent meals to limit gastric overloading, and a low-FODMAP dietary trial for patients with prominent gas and bloating symptoms can substantially improve quality of life. Laxatives or bulking agents are prescribed for constipation. When functional dyspepsia or irritable bowel syndrome coexists — as it frequently does — evidence-based treatments for those conditions (low-dose antidepressants, gut-directed psychological therapies, antispasmodics) are included. Surgical correction — most often a mesentericoplication or fixation of the bowel to the posterior abdominal wall — is confined to a very small number of patients with confirmed, severe enteroptosis causing recurrent partial obstruction or demonstrably worsening quality of life despite adequate conservative therapy. Because causation is difficult to prove, surgical outcomes are variable, and the decision requires careful multidisciplinary deliberation.

medicalTreatments.name: Core muscle rehabilitation with pelvic floor physiotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: A structured programme of core stabilisation exercises — Pilates-based movements, transversus abdominis activation, and postural training — increases resting intra-abdominal pressure and dynamic support of the viscera; combined with pelvic floor work when coexisting prolapse is present.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No randomised trials exist for enteroptosis specifically; in analogous functional abdominal conditions, core rehabilitation improves patient-reported symptoms in approximately 60-70% of participants in cohort studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Laxatives and bulking agents for constipation
medicalTreatments.description: Ispaghula (psyllium husk) or methylcellulose bulking agents, together with osmotic laxatives such as macrogol (polyethylene glycol), improve bowel transit in patients with constipation attributable to mechanical slowing from intestinal displacement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Osmotic laxatives achieve satisfactory bowel function in approximately 70-80% of patients with chronic constipation in the general population; efficacy specific to enteroptosis-related constipation is based on clinical experience rather than dedicated trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Antispasmodics (mebeverine, hyoscine butylbromide)
medicalTreatments.description: Smooth muscle relaxants reduce bowel spasm and cramping pain in patients with coexisting irritable bowel-type symptoms; taken before meals to limit post-prandial cramping.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses of antispasmodics in IBS show symptomatic improvement in approximately 60% of patients compared with 40% for placebo; extrapolated to enteroptosis patients with functional bowel overlap.

medicalTreatments.name: Abdominal support belt or binder
medicalTreatments.description: An external compression garment worn over the lower abdomen physically elevates displaced intestinal loops during standing and walking; provides immediate relief for many patients and confirms the postural nature of symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No controlled trial data exist; clinical observation suggests 50-70% of patients with clear postural symptoms report meaningful improvement with consistent use.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline, nortriptyline)
medicalTreatments.description: At low doses (10-25 mg), these agents modulate visceral pain perception, slow intestinal transit to reduce diarrhoea-predominant symptoms, and improve sleep disrupted by chronic abdominal pain — particularly valuable when functional bowel disease overlaps with enteroptosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Systematic reviews in functional abdominal pain show that tricyclics reduce global symptom scores in approximately 55-60% of patients at four to twelve weeks of treatment.

surgicalOptions.name: Mesentericoplication (Noble plication)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical shortening and plication (folding and suturing) of the small intestinal mesentery to elevate the bowel loops to a higher position; a historical procedure rarely performed now, reserved for patients with severe mechanical symptoms confirmed on dynamic imaging.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Historical series report symptom improvement in 60-70% of carefully selected patients; recurrence over time is common and long-term data are limited.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic intestinal fixation (enteropexia)
surgicalOptions.description: Laparoscopic attachment of displaced intestinal mesentery to the posterior abdominal wall or other fixed anatomical points; a less invasive modern evolution of open mesentericoplication; performed in rare cases at specialised centres.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Short-term outcomes appear similar to open plication; the procedure is uncommon and robust outcome data are lacking, limiting its use to exceptional circumstances.

alternativeTreatments.name: Yoga and Pilates-based exercise programmes
alternativeTreatments.description: Regular practice of yoga postures emphasising core engagement and inversion poses (such as supported shoulderstand) has been reported by patients to reduce the gravitational drag on the bowel; Pilates core activation training is the most evidence-aligned component.

alternativeTreatments.name: Visceral manipulation (osteopathic or physiotherapy)
alternativeTreatments.description: Manual techniques targeting fascial and peritoneal restrictions are used in some physiotherapy traditions to restore normal visceral mobility; evidence from controlled studies is very limited and this approach remains outside mainstream gastroenterology guidelines.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional optimisation to restore body weight
alternativeTreatments.description: In underweight patients, gradual restoration of healthy body weight rebuilds mesenteric fat that helps cushion and support the intestinal loops; this is part of the management of enteroptosis in the post-severe illness or eating disorder context.

whySeeSpecialist: A gastroenterologist should evaluate any patient with chronic, unexplained lower abdominal symptoms that show postural variation, particularly after weight loss or in the context of connective tissue hypermobility. Specialist input is needed to exclude important alternative diagnoses (inflammatory bowel disease, malignancy, coeliac disease, intestinal obstruction) before attributing symptoms to enteroptosis. Where pelvic organ prolapse coexists, a gynaecologist or urogynecologist should be involved. The rare patient considered for surgical correction requires a hepatobiliary or colorectal surgeon with specific experience in abdominal wall and mesenteric anatomy.
doctorSelectionGuide: A consultant gastroenterologist with interest in functional bowel disorders and intestinal motility will provide the most thorough evaluation. Look for a centre with access to dynamic CT or MRI imaging in the upright position, and pelvic floor physiotherapy. For patients with hypermobility syndromes, a physician experienced in Ehlers-Danlos or MCAS presentations will ensure the systemic context is considered alongside the gut symptoms.
preventionStrategies: Avoid rapid or extreme weight loss; gradual reduction at 0.5-1 kg per week allows connective tissue adaptation.
Maintain or build abdominal wall muscle mass through regular core strengthening exercise, which provides dynamic visceral support.
Treat constipation promptly; chronic straining at stool increases intra-abdominal pressure cycles that may worsen mesenteric laxity.
In pregnant women, continue safe ante- and postnatal core rehabilitation to restore abdominal wall support after delivery.
For hypermobility disorder patients, work with a physiotherapist to develop a long-term joint and core stability programme.
lifestyleModifications: Wear an abdominal support belt during prolonged standing or activities that worsen symptoms.
Eat small, frequent meals rather than large portions, which overload a displaced stomach and small bowel and worsen nausea.
Rest in a semi-reclined or lying position for 30-60 minutes after meals to allow intestinal motility under reduced gravitational load.
Avoid prolonged standing without movement; walking engages abdominal and leg muscles that dynamically support visceral position.
Maintain a healthy body weight — neither underweight nor obese — to optimise mesenteric support.
Practise daily core-strengthening exercises as prescribed by a physiotherapist.

recommended: Small, frequent meals (5-6 daily) to reduce post-prandial gastric and intestinal distension
Adequate fluid intake (1.5-2 litres daily) to prevent constipation and promote intestinal transit
Soluble fibre from oats, flaxseed, and psyllium to improve stool consistency without excess fermentation
Low-FODMAP diet trial if gas, bloating, and cramping are dominant symptoms
Gradual reintroduction of fibre if a period of low-fibre eating has worsened constipation
avoid: Large meals that overfill the stomach and cause post-prandial pain and bloating
High-FODMAP foods (onions, legumes, wheat, certain fruits) during symptomatic periods if they worsen gas and urgency
Excessive caffeine, which can worsen intestinal motility and visceral hypersensitivity
Crash diets or very-low-calorie diets that accelerate weight loss and mesenteric fat reduction

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is strongly encouraged, both for its direct effect on abdominal muscle strength and for general health. Core rehabilitation exercises — transversus abdominis activation, pelvic tilts, cat-cow stretches, and low-level Pilates movements — form the specific therapeutic programme and should be performed daily. Swimming and cycling reduce gravitational load compared to running and are often better tolerated in the early rehabilitation phase. Heavy resistance lifting that increases intra-abdominal pressure should be introduced gradually, guided by a physiotherapist. Inversion-based yoga poses can provide temporary relief of symptoms for motivated patients.
dailyManagement: Practise core rehabilitation exercises every morning before prolonged standing.
Wear abdominal support when anticipating extended standing or walking.
Take bulk-forming or osmotic laxatives consistently as prescribed to prevent constipation.
Rest lying flat for 30 minutes at midday if symptoms peak in the afternoon.
Keep a symptom diary to identify food, posture, and activity triggers and discuss them at clinic follow-up.
Attend pelvic floor physiotherapy appointments consistently — improvements in this condition take weeks to months.
prognosis: The prognosis of enteroptosis is generally favourable. The majority of patients with mild-to-moderate symptoms achieve substantial improvement with conservative management — core rehabilitation, abdominal support, and dietary adjustments — over a period of weeks to months. The condition is not progressive in most individuals, and spontaneous improvement occurs in some patients as abdominal muscle mass and tone are restored. The subset of patients with severe chronic symptoms, connective tissue disorders, or coexisting pelvic organ prolapse require longer management timelines and more intensive physiotherapy. The very small minority who undergo surgical correction have variable outcomes, with approximately 60-70% reporting improvement in historical series. There is no evidence that enteroptosis per se shortens life or significantly increases the risk of serious complications; the main burden is chronic discomfort and reduced quality of life, both of which are addressable with the measures described.
recoveryTimeline: With a structured physiotherapy programme, noticeable improvement in core strength and reduction in daily symptoms typically occurs over six to twelve weeks. Full benefit from core rehabilitation — sustained reduction in dragging discomfort, bloating, and constipation — generally takes three to six months of consistent effort. Dietary changes can improve bloating and constipation within two to four weeks. After surgical correction, recovery from laparoscopic procedures takes two to four weeks before return to light activity, with core rehabilitation resuming at six weeks post-operatively.
complications: Chronic constipation — the most common complication; mechanical slowing of intestinal transit from mesenteric traction leads to sluggish bowel habit, straining, and haemorrhoids if not managed actively.

Partial intestinal obstruction — in severe enteroptosis, a kinked or adhesion-tethered displaced loop may intermittently obstruct intestinal transit, causing episodic vomiting and abdominal pain requiring urgent assessment.

Nutritional deficiency — early satiety, nausea, and fear of exacerbating symptoms can lead to reduced caloric and nutrient intake, contributing to further weight loss and perpetuating the mesenteric laxity.

Psychological impact — chronic unexplained abdominal pain with limited medical recognition can cause health anxiety, depression, and social withdrawal; integrated psychological support is an important part of management.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Digestive Diseases — Bowel Health
supportResources.description: Authoritative U.S. patient information on functional and structural bowel conditions from the National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: NHS — Pelvic organ prolapse
supportResources.description: UK NHS guidance on organ prolapse — a related condition sharing risk factors and management approaches with enteroptosis.

supportResources.name: The Ehlers-Danlos Society
supportResources.description: Patient support and medical resources for connective tissue hypermobility disorders, which predispose to visceral ptosis including enteroptosis.

sources.title: Glenard F. Les ptoses viscerales (stomach, intestines, kidney, liver, spleen). 1899 (foundational description of visceroptosis/Glenard's disease)

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome with constipation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IBS-C causes chronic constipation and abdominal pain without any anatomical displacement on imaging; enteroptosis is an imaging finding with postural symptom variation, whereas IBS symptoms do not consistently change with body position.

confusedWithConditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Functional dyspepsia causes upper abdominal bloating, early satiety, and nausea from altered gastric emptying or sensory dysfunction without organ displacement; it is diagnosed by symptom criteria after excluding structural pathology, whereas enteroptosis is confirmed on upright imaging.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastroparesis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gastroparesis causes nausea, vomiting, and early satiety from delayed gastric emptying confirmed by a gastric emptying scintigraphy study; the mechanism is neuromuscular, not mechanical displacement.

relatedConditions.name: Gastroparesis
relatedConditions.relevance: Gastroptosis — downward displacement of the stomach — commonly coexists with enteroptosis in visceroptosis and produces overlapping symptoms of nausea and early satiety.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic idiopathic constipation
relatedConditions.relevance: Enteroptosis is an important structural contributor to chronic constipation via altered mesenteric tension and intestinal transit, and the two conditions require coordinated management.

relatedConditions.name: IBS with constipation
relatedConditions.relevance: Functional bowel symptoms from enteroptosis closely overlap with IBS-C and the two commonly coexist, complicating diagnosis and management.

relatedConditions.name: Mixed IBS
relatedConditions.relevance: The alternating diarrhoea and constipation pattern of mixed IBS is also reported in enteroptosis patients with disordered intestinal motility, and the two diagnoses may apply simultaneously.

faqs.question: What is enteroptosis?
faqs.answer: Enteroptosis is the downward sagging of the small intestinal loops within the abdominal cavity, caused by weakness of the mesenteric ligaments that normally keep the bowel in position. It is more noticeable when standing and typically causes lower abdominal heaviness, bloating, and constipation.

faqs.question: What causes enteroptosis?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are a constitutionally long or lax mesentery, rapid or significant weight loss that removes supporting fat, weakness of the abdominal wall muscles, and multiple pregnancies. Connective tissue disorders such as hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome are also recognised causes.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of enteroptosis?
faqs.answer: Typical symptoms are a chronic dragging heaviness in the lower abdomen that worsens when standing and eases when lying down, bloating that builds through the day, constipation, lower abdominal cramping, nausea after meals, and early satiety. Symptoms are reliably worse upright and better supine, a feature that distinguishes enteroptosis from most other causes.

faqs.question: How is enteroptosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Enteroptosis is diagnosed on upright abdominal X-ray or CT scan, which shows the small intestinal loops positioned abnormally low — below the level of the iliac crests. The comparison between upright and supine images confirms postural organ descent. Endoscopy and blood tests are performed to exclude other conditions with similar symptoms.

faqs.question: Does enteroptosis require surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is rarely needed. The great majority of patients improve with core muscle strengthening exercises, abdominal support garments, dietary adjustments, and treatment of constipation. Surgical correction by mesentericoplication or intestinal fixation is confined to a very small number of patients with severe mechanical obstruction symptoms unresponsive to all conservative measures.

faqs.question: What exercises help enteroptosis?
faqs.answer: Core stabilisation exercises targeting the transversus abdominis muscle — performed lying down to remove gravitational load — are the mainstay of physical therapy. Pilates-based programmes, pelvic tilts, and cat-cow movements build the abdominal wall support that reduces bowel descent. A physiotherapist can design a programme appropriate to current fitness level.

faqs.question: Can an abdominal belt help enteroptosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. A firm abdominal support belt worn during prolonged standing mechanically lifts the displaced intestines and provides immediate relief of the dragging heaviness and bloating for most patients. It is a useful short-term measure and confirms the postural mechanical nature of symptoms, though it does not treat the underlying laxity.

faqs.question: Is enteroptosis the same as visceroptosis?
faqs.answer: Enteroptosis refers specifically to downward displacement of the small intestine. Visceroptosis is the broader term for prolapse of any or all abdominal organs — stomach, kidneys, liver, colon, or spleen — and is sometimes called Glenard's disease. Enteroptosis is the most commonly symptomatic component of visceroptosis.

faqs.question: Can weight gain improve enteroptosis?
faqs.answer: In underweight patients where rapid weight loss removed the mesenteric fat cushion, gradual restoration of a healthy body weight can partly restore visceral support and reduce symptoms. Crash dieting or very rapid weight loss should be avoided. Healthy weight restoration combined with core muscle strengthening gives the best outcome.

faqs.question: Can pregnancy cause enteroptosis?
faqs.answer: Pregnancy and childbirth stretch the anterior abdominal wall and pelvic floor, reducing the structural support of the abdominal organs. Post-partum enteroptosis can develop, particularly after multiple deliveries. Postnatal core rehabilitation is important both to restore abdominal wall function and to prevent or improve visceroptosis symptoms.

faqs.question: Is enteroptosis a serious condition?
faqs.answer: Enteroptosis is not life-threatening in the vast majority of cases. The main concern is chronic discomfort and reduced quality of life from pain, bloating, and constipation. Rare complications include partial intestinal obstruction. Most patients achieve significant improvement with conservative treatment and the condition does not shorten life expectancy.

faqs.question: Does enteroptosis cause constipation?
faqs.answer: Yes. The downward and sometimes kinked positioning of intestinal loops can slow transit and increase mechanical resistance to stool passage. Constipation is one of the most common symptoms. Treatment includes bulking agents, osmotic laxatives, adequate fluid intake, and core exercises that improve bowel motility by strengthening the abdominal wall.

faqs.question: What diet should I follow for enteroptosis?
faqs.answer: Small, frequent meals reduce post-prandial distension. Adequate fluids and soluble fibre (psyllium, oats) improve constipation. A low-FODMAP diet trial can reduce bloating from fermentation. Crash dieting and large meals that overfill a displaced stomach should be avoided. Working with a dietitian is helpful for persistent symptoms.

faqs.question: Can enteroptosis cause nausea?
faqs.answer: Yes. Gastroptosis — downward stomach displacement — frequently coexists with enteroptosis, altering gastric emptying and causing nausea and early satiety, particularly after meals. Eating small portions, avoiding lying flat immediately after eating, and postural adjustments (sitting upright) during meals can reduce meal-related nausea.

faqs.question: How long does it take for enteroptosis to improve?
faqs.answer: With consistent core rehabilitation and dietary management, most patients notice improvement within 6-12 weeks. Full benefit — sustained reduction in dragging discomfort, bloating, and constipation — typically takes 3-6 months of regular physiotherapy. Progress is gradual and early compliance with the exercise programme is the strongest predictor of outcome.

faqs.question: Is enteroptosis linked to connective tissue disorders?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Marfan syndrome, and other connective tissue disorders cause generalised ligamentous laxity that extends to the peritoneal folds supporting the bowel. People with these conditions are at higher risk of enteroptosis and visceroptosis, and their management must account for the systemic context of their hypermobility.

faqs.question: Can lying down relieve enteroptosis symptoms?
faqs.answer: Yes, and this postural change is one of the most diagnostically informative features of enteroptosis. The gravitational descent of the intestines is reversed in the supine position, relieving mesenteric traction and reducing pain, bloating, and heaviness. Patients whose abdominal symptoms reliably improve within minutes of lying flat should mention this to their gastroenterologist.

faqs.question: Will enteroptosis get worse over time?
faqs.answer: Enteroptosis is not typically a progressive disease in most patients. With appropriate core strengthening and weight management, symptoms often stabilise or improve. In patients with connective tissue disorders or ongoing muscle loss, gradual worsening can occur without intervention. Regular physiotherapy review and proactive management prevent deterioration.

faqs.question: What is Glenard's disease?
faqs.answer: Glenard's disease, described by French physician Frantz Glenard in 1885, is the historical term for visceroptosis — the generalised downward displacement of the abdominal organs. Enteroptosis (small intestine ptosis) is one component. The term is rarely used in modern clinical medicine but appears in older texts and some specialist discussions.

faqs.question: Can physiotherapy cure enteroptosis?
faqs.answer: Physiotherapy does not alter the underlying mesenteric anatomy but significantly reduces symptoms by strengthening the abdominal wall muscles that dynamically compensate for ligamentous laxity. Many patients achieve a quality of life equivalent to those without the condition with sustained physiotherapy. A structured, supervised programme with a physiotherapist experienced in core rehabilitation gives the best results.

metaDescription: Enteroptosis is intestinal downward displacement causing heaviness, bloating and constipation worse when standing. Learn causes and treatment. Doctor-reviewed.
